title: Procs: Saved Blocks 💾
description: Don't repeat yourself! Save your blocks as variables using Procs.

slides.title: The Problem with Blocks 🤷
slides.content:
Blocks are incredibly useful, but they have one massive flaw: they are completely anonymous.

Once a block finishes running, it vanishes into the ether. If you want to use the exact same block code on three different arrays, you have to type it out three separate times.

That severely violates the D.R.Y (Don't Repeat Yourself) rule!

slides.title: Enter the Proc 💾
slides.content:
A **Proc** (short for Procedure) is simply a Block that you have physically saved into a Variable.

By saving the block to a variable, you give it a name! This means you can call it over and over again without ever retyping the code.

```ruby
# Creating a Proc and saving it to a variable
say_hi = Proc.new { puts "Hi there!" }
```

slides.title: Calling a Proc
slides.content: To execute the code stored inside of a Proc, you just grab the variable name and attach `.call` to the end of it.
slides.codeSnippet:
greet = Proc.new { puts "Welcome to the program!" }

# Calling our saved block over and over
greet.call
greet.call
greet.call

quizData.question: At its core, what actually is a 'Proc'?

options.text: A block of code that has been saved into a variable name so it can be reused.

quizData.explanation: Proc stands for Procedure. It is exactly identical to a block of code, except you saved it so it no longer vanishes.

slides.title: Passing Procs 🏈
slides.content:
You can pass your shiny new Proc into an iterator (like `.each`) just like you would a block! 

However, because it's a variable and not an anonymous block, you must put an ampersand `&` in front of it to tell the method *"Hey, treat this variable like a block of code."*

`array.each(&my_proc)`

slides.title: The Magic Ampersand
slides.content: Run this! Notice how clean our `.each` call is because all the heavy logic is tucked away inside the Proc.
slides.codeSnippet:
cube_it = Proc.new { |x| puts x ** 3 }

numbers = [2, 3, 4]

# The ampersand turns the variable back into a block
numbers.each(&cube_it)

quizData.question: Which symbol must you place in front of a Proc variable so an iterator method knows to treat it as a block of code?
quizData.explanation: The `&` operator converts a Proc variable directly down into a block that the `.each` iterator can magically execute.
